explicit_text: I served in the United States Army during World War II after being rejected by the Marine Corps due to color blindness. I was part of the 80th Infantry Division and stayed with them throughout the war. My training and combat experiences were all under the Army, from basic training camps in the US to fighting in Europe.

explicit_text: I was assigned to the 80th Infantry Division during the war and stayed with them the entire time. We fought in Europe, including during the Battle of the Bulge in the winter of 1944. The division was featured in a magazine I still have, showing the bitter cold conditions we endured.

explicit_text: I initially enlisted in the United States Marine Corps when I was 17, early in my senior year of high school. However, I was rejected during the eye exam because I was color blind. The examiner suggested I would make a fine infantryman, which led me to join the Army instead.

explicit_text: After enlisting in the Army, I was first sent to Camp Atterbury, Indiana. I stayed there only about four days before being transferred. It was the first stop in my training journey before heading to other camps.

explicit_text: I spent some time at Camp Joseph T. Robinson in Arkansas, which was a relatively new camp with tar paper shacks. There were about 79,000 of us there, and I remember the cold mornings where we had to build fires ourselves to heat the shanties. A sergeant there really boosted our spirits and made the experience more bearable.

explicit_text: I came from Kentucky and had never traveled more than 30 miles from home before the war. After basic training, I got a seven-day delay in route to visit home in Kentucky before heading east to Fort Meade and eventually overseas. Kentucky was my starting point before all the changes of the war.

explicit_text: After leaving Kentucky, I traveled to Fort Meade, Maryland, as part of my journey to deployment. From there, I went on to Camp Kilmer in New Jersey and then Camp Shanks in New York. Fort Meade was one of the key stops before heading overseas.

explicit_text: Camp Shanks in New York was the last camp I stayed at before boarding the ship to Europe. It was very secretive at the time, and late one night we were ordered to board the RMS Aquitania. This camp marked the final step before crossing the Atlantic.

explicit_text: We boarded the RMS Aquitania late at night with about 10,000 soldiers on board to cross the Atlantic. The journey took about seven days, and we were warned about German U-boats and how to behave if torpedoed. The ship was fast and well-armed, and the crossing was tense, especially when we passed a burning ship in the North Atlantic.

explicit_text: We arrived in London at night, and that very night the city was bombed. That experience put me off London, so we moved on quickly to southern England to prepare for the invasion of France. It was a frightening introduction to the war in Europe.

explicit_text: We crossed into France on open metal boats after preparing in England. The shore was completely destroyed when we landed, showing the heavy fighting that had already taken place. Although I wasn't there for D-Day itself, we moved quickly to the front lines after landing.

explicit_text: I fought during the Battle of the Bulge in the bitter winter of 1944. We marched long distances in the snow, carrying heavy loads and enduring freezing temperatures. It was one of the toughest campaigns I experienced, and I still have a magazine photo of my division from that time.

explicit_text: Adolf Hitler was the German leader during the war we fought in Europe. His forces built the Siegfried Line and launched the offensive that led to the Battle of the Bulge. Facing his army was a daunting challenge throughout my combat experience.
